questions.prompt: What axes should an agent eval cover beyond just final output quality?

choices.text: Tool selection (right tools, right order, right args), efficiency (step count within budget), and safety (approvals respected, no destructive actions outside the gate). Each axis catches a different failure mode — and each can regress independently of the others.

questions.explanation: Final output is necessary but not sufficient. A 12-step agent producing a correct answer might be 2.5× more expensive than the 5-step version doing the same — output quality wouldn't catch it. Multi-axis evals catch tool selection regressions, efficiency drift, and safety bypasses that end-to-end scoring would miss.

questions.prompt: Your agent eval cases pass single-run but flap on re-run (sometimes pass, sometimes fail). What's the right approach?

choices.text: Loosen the rubrics until they always pass.

Run each case 3-5× and require majority pass. Pin temperature to 0 for eval runs. Track flakiness as its own signal — cases that flap randomly tell you about agent variance. Don't write maximally generous rubrics that hide real regressions; find the floor of acceptable performance and grade against that.

questions.explanation: Agent variance is real. Multi-run majority + temperature 0 reduces flakiness. Flaky cases themselves are signal — high variance on a case means the agent is on a knife edge there. Don't paper over with generous rubrics; address the underlying variance.
